content.text: After the model outputs logits for the next token,
sampling strategies

decide which token to append. Temperature, top-k, and top-p (nucleus) reshape the probability distribution before drawing a token. Agent builders tune these knobs to balance creative exploration against reliable, parseable tool output.
What it is
Sampling converts model logits into a stochastic choice at each decode step.
Greedy
picks argmax;
temperature scaling
flattens or sharpens the distribution;
top-k
keeps only the k highest-probability tokens;
top-p (nucleus)
keeps the smallest set whose cumulative probability exceeds p. Most production APIs expose these as generation parameters layered on the same softmax output.
Why it matters

Agents that call tools need JSON and function names to be stable. High temperature increases variance—useful for brainstorming agents, harmful for schema-constrained steps. Low temperature with top-p near 1.0 yields near-deterministic completions. Misconfigured sampling is a common root cause of flaky agent runs that pass in testing but fail in production.
How it works
Temperature scaling

Logits are divided by temperature T before softmax. T → 0 approaches argmax; T > 1 spreads probability mass to unlikely tokens. Typical agent defaults: T = 0–0.3 for tool calls, T = 0.7–1.0 for open-ended drafting.
Top-k filtering

Sort tokens by probability, zero out all but the top k, renormalize, then sample. k = 1 is greedy. k = 40–100 is common for chat. Too small k can truncate valid rare tokens (e.g., uncommon API enum values).
Top-p (nucleus) sampling

Sort by descending probability, accumulate until sum ≥ p, discard the rest, renormalize, sample. Adapts candidate set size to model confidence—few candidates when one token dominates, many when the distribution is flat. Often combined with temperature; top-k and top-p are usually not both needed at aggressive settings.

import numpy as np

def softmax(logits):
    e = np.exp(logits - np.max(logits))
    return e / e.sum()

def sample_top_p(logits, p=0.9, temperature=0.8):
    probs = softmax(logits / temperature)
    sorted_idx = np.argsort(probs)[::-1]
    sorted_probs = probs[sorted_idx]
    cumulative = np.cumsum(sorted_probs)
    cutoff = np.searchsorted(cumulative, p) + 1
    mask = np.zeros_like(probs)
    mask[sorted_idx[:cutoff]] = sorted_probs[:cutoff]
    mask /= mask.sum()
    return np.random.choice(len(probs), p=mask)
Benefits
Top-p adapts to per-step uncertainty without fixing k globally
Temperature gives a single intuitive creativity dial
Low-variance settings improve reproducibility for structured agent outputs
Stochastic sampling enables diverse rollouts for planning and self-consistency
Trade-offs
Stochastic sampling complicates testing unless seed and temperature are fixed
Aggressive top-k can block valid low-frequency tokens needed in code or IDs
Temperature and top-p interact—tuning one in isolation can mislead
No sampling strategy fixes factual errors; it only changes which plausible token is chosen
When to use

Use low temperature and moderate top-p (0.9–1.0) for tool selection, JSON generation, and classification steps in agent pipelines. Use higher temperature for ideation, multiple candidate plans, or synthetic data generation. Disable sampling (temperature 0, or greedy) when exact reproducibility is required for audits or regression tests.
Agentic AI connection
Multi-step agents often use
per-step sampling profiles

: deterministic for tool routing, slightly stochastic for user-facing prose. Some frameworks expose separate generation configs per node in a graph. Self-consistency (sample N completions, majority vote) relies on deliberate temperature > 0 for reasoning steps.
Common mistakes
Setting temperature 0 but leaving top-p at 0.1, unexpectedly restricting the candidate set
Using high temperature on the same model call that must emit valid JSON
Assuming provider defaults are safe for agents—always set explicitly per task type
Confusing API "temperature" docs with the separate generation-parameters topic; here the focus is the algorithm applied to logits
Best practices
Document sampling settings per agent node in config, not only in prompts
Log chosen parameters alongside completions for debugging flaky runs
Benchmark tool-call success rate across temperature sweeps before production
Prefer top-p over top-k when vocabulary tail matters (code, UUIDs, rare enums)
Key takeaways
Sampling reshapes the next-token distribution before selection
Temperature controls sharpness; top-k and top-p restrict the candidate set
Agents need low-variance sampling for structured outputs
Top-p adapts candidate count to model confidence at each step
Explicit per-task sampling beats provider defaults for reliable agents
